blocks:
Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمُزَّمِّلُ (١)
O you wrapped up in your mantle! (1)
قُمِ ٱلَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٢)
Stand vigil through the night, except for a little [of it], (2)
نِّصْفَهُۥٓ أَوِ ٱنقُصْ مِنْهُ قَلِيلًا (٣)
a half, or reduce a little from that (3)
أَوْ زِدْ عَلَيْهِ وَرَتِّلِ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَرْتِيلًا (٤)
or add to it, and recite the Quran in a measured tone. (4)
إِنَّا سَنُلْقِى عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا (٥)
Indeed soon We shall cast on you a weighty discourse. (5)
إِنَّ نَاشِئَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ هِىَ أَشَدُّ وَطْـًٔا وَأَقْوَمُ قِيلًا (٦)
Indeed the watch of the night is firmer in tread and more upright in respect to speech, (6)
إِنَّ لَكَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ سَبْحًا طَوِيلًا (٧)
for indeed during the day you have drawn-out engagements. (7)
وَٱذْكُرِ ٱسْمَ رَبِّكَ وَتَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلًا (٨)
So celebrate the Name of your Lord and dedicate yourself to Him with total dedication. (8)
رَّبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَٱتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلًا (٩)
Lord of the east and the west, there is no god except Him; so take Him for your trustee, (9)
وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَٱهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْرًا جَمِيلًا (١٠)
and be patient over what they say, and distance yourself from them in a graceful manner. (10)
وَذَرْنِى وَٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ أُو۟لِى ٱلنَّعْمَةِ وَمَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلًا (١١)
Leave Me [to deal] with the deniers, the opulent, and give them a little respite. (11)
إِنَّ لَدَيْنَآ أَنكَالًا وَجَحِيمًا (١٢)
Indeed with Us are heavy fetters and a fierce fire, (12)
وَطَعَامًا ذَا غُصَّةٍ وَعَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٣)
and a food that chokes [those who eat it], and a painful punishment [prepared for] (13)
يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ وَكَانَتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَثِيبًا مَّهِيلًا (١٤)
the day when the earth and the mountains will quake, and the mountains will be like dunes of shifting sand. (14)
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ رَسُولًا شَـٰهِدًا عَلَيْكُمْ كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ رَسُولًا (١٥)
Indeed We have sent to you an apostle, to be a witness to you, just as We sent an apostle to Pharaoh. (15)
فَعَصَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ أَخْذًا وَبِيلًا (١٦)
But Pharaoh disobeyed the apostle; so We seized him with a terrible seizing. (16)
فَكَيْفَ تَتَّقُونَ إِن كَفَرْتُمْ يَوْمًا يَجْعَلُ ٱلْوِلْدَٰنَ شِيبًا (١٧)
So, if you disbelieve, how will you avoid the day, which will make children white-headed, (17)
ٱلسَّمَآءُ مُنفَطِرٌۢ بِهِۦ ۚ كَانَ وَعْدُهُۥ مَفْعُولًا (١٨)
and wherein the sky will be rent apart? His promise is bound to be fulfilled. (18)
إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦ تَذْكِرَةٌ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا (١٩)
This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way toward his Lord. (19)

(Commentary)

The Surah commands the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to stand in prayer during the night and to perform the prayer (الصلاة) therein, so that he may prepare himself thereby to receive the weight of what will be cast upon him of the "heavy word" (القول الثقيل) and the inspired Quran. It also commands him to be patient with what they say regarding him—that he is a poet, a soothsayer, or a madman, and so forth—and to forsake them with a "graceful parting" (هجرا جميلا). The Surah contains a threat and a warning to the disbelievers (الكفار) and generalizes the ruling to the rest of the believers (المؤمنين). At its conclusion, there is a mitigation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers.

The Surah is Meccan and is among the ancient Surahs revealed at the beginning of the Prophetic mission (البعثة); it has even been said that it is the second or third Surah revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ — O you wrapped up in your robes! (73:1). The word al-muzzammil (المزمل) is with a doubled zay and mim, originally al-mutazammil (المتزمل), which is the active participle (اسم فاعل) of al-tazammul (التزمل), meaning to wrap oneself in a garment for sleep or the like. Its apparent meaning is that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had wrapped himself in a garment for sleep when the revelation (الوحي) descended upon him, and he was addressed as "the wrapped one" (المزمل).

There is neither disparagement nor praise in this address, as some have imagined. However, it is possible to infer from the context of the verses that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had been met in his mission with mockery, derision, and harm, causing him grief for the sake of Allah. Thus, he wrapped himself in a garment to sleep to ward off the distress. He was then addressed as "the wrapped one" and commanded to stand in the night, to pray therein, and to be patient with what they say, similar to His saying, the Exalted: اسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلاةِ — And take recourse in patience and prayer (2:153). This conveyed that he must resist great distress and bitter calamities through prayer and patience, not through wrapping himself up and sleeping.

It has also been said that the meaning is: "O you wrapped in the mantle of Prophethood," meaning the one bearing its burdens; however, there is no evidence for this from the linguistic aspect.

His saying, the Exalted: قُمِ اللَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً نِصْفَهُ أَوِ انْقُصْ مِنْهُ قَلِيلاً أَوْ زِدْ عَلَيْهِ وَرَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلاً — Stand vigil through the night, except a little, a half, or reduce a little from that, or add to it, and recite the Quran in a measured tone. (73:2-4). What is meant by "standing the night" (قيام الليل) is standing therein for prayer. Thus, "the night" (الليل) is the direct object (مفعول به) by way of extension (توسعا), as in the phrase "I entered the house." It has also been said that the object of "stand" (قم) is omitted, and "the night" is an adverb of time (منصوب على الظرفية), the meaning being: "Stand for prayer during the night." His saying, "except a little" (إِلَّا قَلِيلاً), is an exception from the night.

His saying, "a half, or reduce a little from that, or add to it" (نِصْفَهُ أَوِ انْقُصْ مِنْهُ قَلِيلاً أَوْ زِدْ عَلَيْهِ), appears from the context to be an appositive (بدل) for "the night, except a little," which is the object of the obligation to stand. The pronouns in "from it" (مِنْهُ) and "upon it" (عَلَيْهِ) refer to the "half," while the pronoun in "its half" (نِصْفَهُ) refers to the "night." The meaning is: "Stand half the night, or decrease a little from the half, or add a little to the half." The choice between the three is for option (التخيير); he was given the choice between standing half the night, standing slightly less than half, or standing slightly more than half.

It has also been said that "its half" (نِصْفَهُ) is an appositive for the excepted part, namely "a little" (قَلِيلاً). The meaning would then be: "Stand the night except for its half, or decrease from the half a little (thus standing slightly more than half), or add to the half (thus standing slightly less than half)." In this case, the appositive clause would resolve the ambiguity of the excepted part (المستثنى) through correspondence (بالمطابقة) and the ambiguity of the whole from which the exception is made (المستثنى منه) by implication (بالالتزام), which is the reverse of the previous view.

Although both views lead to the same result, the former view is more immediate to the mind. This is because the need to resolve ambiguity regarding the object of the ruling (متعلق الحكم) is more primary than the need to resolve ambiguity regarding its subordinates and attachments. Thus, "its half" etc., being an appositive for "the night"—which necessitates resolving the ambiguity of the object of the obligation through correspondence—is more immediate to the mind than it being an appositive for "a little."

It has also been said that "its half" is an appositive for "the night," but "the little" refers to a small number of nights rather than a small portion of the night. The meaning would be: "Stand half the night, or decrease a little from it, or add to it, except for a few nights," which are the nights of excuse due to illness, being overcome by sleep, or the like. There is no harm in this view, but the first view is more immediate to the mind.

His saying, وَرَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلاً — and recite the Quran in a measured tone (73:4). The "measured recitation" (ترتيل) of the Quran is its recitation by articulating its letters clearly and sequentially. The sentence is coordinated (معطوفة) with His saying, "Stand the night," meaning: "Stand the night and read the Quran with measured recitation."

It appears that what is meant by the measured recitation of the Quran is its recitation within the prayer, or it refers to the prayer itself. The Exalted has expressed the prayer with a similar expression in His saying: أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ إِلى غَسَقِ اللَّيْلِ وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً — Maintain the prayer from the sun’s decline till the darkness of the night, and [observe particularly] the dawn recital. Indeed the dawn recital is attended [by angels]. (17:78). It has also been said that the meaning is the obligation of reciting the Quran apart from the prayer.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلاً ثَقِيلاً — Soon We shall cast on you a weighty word. (73:5). Weight (الثقل) is a physical quality whose characteristic is that it is difficult to carry a heavy body or move it from place to place. It is sometimes used metaphorically for meanings (المعاني) when it is difficult for the soul to bear them or when it cannot endure them. Thus, it is sometimes attributed to speech (القول) in terms of its meaning, being considered "heavy" because it contains a meaning that is difficult for the soul to perceive, or that it cannot grasp, or that it feels constrained in receiving—such as subtle scientific insights when presented to common understandings. Or, it may be because it contains realities (حقائق) that are difficult to realize (التحقق بها), or obligations (تكاليف) that are difficult to perform and maintain.

The Quran is a divine word that is "heavy" in both senses. As for the reception of its meaning, it is a divine speech taken from the presence of Majesty and Grandeur; it is not received except by a soul purified from every defilement, detached from every cause except Allah, the Glorified. It is a "mighty book" (كتاب عزيز) having an outward (ظهر) and an inward (بطن), a revelation (تنزيل) and an interpretation (تأويل), being an "exposition of all things" (تبيانا لكل شيء). Its weight was witnessed in the state of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by the intense feverish state (البرحاء) and the semblance of fainting that would overcome him, as reported in the widely transmitted narrations (الأخبار المستفيضة).

As for the realization of the reality of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the doctrinal realities that follow it, it suffices to point to its weight by His saying, the Exalted: لَوْ أَنْزَلْنا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلى جَبَلٍ لَرَأَيْتَهُ خاشِعاً مُتَصَدِّعاً مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللهِ وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ — Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have surely seen it humbled [and] rent asunder out of the fear of Allah. We draw such parables for mankind so that they may reflect. (59:21), and His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآناً سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ الْجِبالُ أَوْ قُطِّعَتْ بِهِ الْأَرْضُ أَوْ كُلِّمَ بِهِ الْمَوْتى — If only it were a Quran whereby the mountains could be moved, or the earth could be toured, or the dead could be spoken to... (13:31).

As for the performance of what it contains regarding the matter of the mission (الدعوة), the establishment of the rites of the upright religion (الدين الحنيف), and making it prevail over all religions, this is witnessed by the calamities and trials he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) encountered in the way of Allah and the harm he suffered for the sake of Allah, as evidenced by the Quranic verses recounting what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) faced from the polytheists (المشركين), the disbelievers, the hypocrites (المنافقين), and those in whose hearts is a sickness, in terms of various types of harm, mockery, and harshness.

Thus, in His saying, "Soon We shall cast on you a weighty word," what is meant by the "weighty word" is the Great Quran, as is immediate to the mind from the context of these verses revealed at the beginning of the mission; this is how the commentators (المفسرون) have explained it.

The verse serves as the rationale (التعليل) for the ruling indicated by His saying, "Stand the night," etc. According to the context—and the address is specific to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—it signifies that his being commanded to stand the night and turn therein toward Him, the Exalted, through the night prayer (صلاة الليل), is a preparation and readiness for the honor of proximity (القرب), the nobility of presence (الحضور), and the casting of a weighty word. Thus, standing the night is the path leading to this noble station. The Glorified has counted the night prayer as a path to Him in His upcoming saying: إِنَّ هذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً — This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way to his Lord. (73:19).

The Glorified added a promise beyond what is in this verse in His saying: وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَتَهَجَّدْ بِهِ نافِلَةً لَكَ عَسى أَنْ يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقاماً مَحْمُوداً — And keep vigil for a part of the night, as a supererogatory [devotion] for you. It may be that your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station. (17:79). The meaning of the "praiseworthy station" (المقام المحمود) has already been discussed in the commentary on that verse.

Since part of the weight of the Quran is its weight in terms of realizing its realities and in terms of responding to the laws (الشرائع) and rulings (الأحكام) it invites to, it is heavy upon the nation (الأمة) just as it is heavy upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The meaning of the verse is: "We shall reveal to you a word that will be heavy upon you and upon your nation." As for its weight upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), it is due to the difficulty in realizing its realities, the trial of the messengership (الرسالة) and the subsequent harm for the sake of Allah, the abandonment of rest and ease, the struggle against the self (مجاهدة النفس), and the total devotion (الانقطاع) to Allah, in addition to the exertion involved in receiving it from the source of revelation (الوحي). As for its weight upon his nation, it is because they share with him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) the necessity of realizing its realities, following its commands and prohibitions, and observing its limits—each group according to its capacity.

The scholars have other views regarding the meaning of the "weight" of the Quran:

  1. It is "heavy" in the sense of being of great importance (عظيم الشأن), solid, and firm, as it is said: "This is a speech that has weight" when it is perfectly placed.
  2. It is heavy in the Balance (الميزان) on the Day of Resurrection, literally or metaphorically, meaning the abundance of reward for it.
  3. It is heavy upon the disbelievers and hypocrites due to its inimitability (الإعجاز) and the threats it contains.
  4. Its weight is a metaphor for its permanence throughout time, for that which is heavy tends to remain and stay fixed in its place.
  5. Other views besides these. While the mentioned views are not problematic in themselves, the view previously mentioned is the apparent one that is immediate to the mind.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ ناشِئَةَ اللَّيْلِ هِيَ أَشَدُّ وَطْئاً وَأَقْوَمُ قِيلاً إِنَّ لَكَ فِي النَّهارِ سَبْحاً طَوِيلاً — Indeed the watch of the night is more effective and more conducive to right speech. Indeed during the day you have much to do. (73:6-7). The first verse serves as the rationale for choosing the night as the time for this prayer, and the second verse serves as the rationale for leaving the day and turning away from it, just as the preceding verse—namely His saying, "Soon We shall cast on you a weighty word"—served as the rationale for the legislation of the essence of this prayer.

So His saying, "Indeed the watch of the night is more effective and more conducive to right speech": al-nashi'ah (الناشئة) is either a verbal noun (مصدر) like al-aqibah (العاقبة) and al-afiyah (العافية), meaning the "arising" (النشأة), which is the occurrence and formation; or it is an active participle (اسم فاعل) from "arising" (النشأة) appended to its described object. In any case, what is meant by it is the night. The application of "occurrence" (الحادثة) to the night is like its application to other parts of creation. It has also been said that it refers to the prayer during the night. "Treading" (الوطء) is placing the foot upon the ground; its being "more effective" (أشد وطأ) is a metaphor for being more firm of foot due to the purity of the soul and its not being clouded by daytime distractions. It has also been said that "treading" (الوطء) refers to the correspondence (مواطاة) of the heart with the tongue, which is supported by the variant reading wita'an (وطاء). What is meant by it being "more conducive to right speech" (أقوم قيلا) is being more firm in speech and more correct due to the presence of the heart and the stillness of sounds.

The meaning is that the occurrence of the night, or the prayer during the night, is more firm of foot—or more intense in the correspondence of the heart with the tongue—and more firm and correct in speech, because Allah made the night a time of rest (سكنا) that entails the detachment of man from the distractions of livelihood toward himself and the emptiness of his mind.

His saying, إِنَّ لَكَ فِي النَّهارِ سَبْحاً طَوِيلاً — Indeed during the day you have much to do (73:7). al-Sabh (السبح) is rapid movement in water. "Long swimming" (السبح الطويل) during the day is a metaphor for immersion in the important matters of livelihood and the various types of movement in fulfilling the needs of life.

The meaning is: You have many distractions during the day that occupy you, being all-encompassing and leaving you no free time to engage in complete turning toward your Lord and total devotion to Him through His remembrance; therefore, you must attend to the night and the prayer therein.

It has been said the meaning is: You have free time during the day for your sleep, the management of your livelihood, and attending to your needs; therefore, keep vigil (تهجد) during the night.

It has also been said the meaning is: You have free time during the day, so if you miss anything from the night, you can make it up during the day and compensate for it therein. Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ خِلْفَةً لِمَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرادَ شُكُوراً — It is He who made the night and the day follow each other for him who desires to take admonition or desires to be grateful. (25:62).

The meaning we have presented is more suitable for the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ وَتَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلاً — And celebrate the Name of your Lord, and devote yourself to Him with exclusive devotion. (73:8). It appears that this describes the night prayer; thus, it is like an explanatory coordination (عطف تفسيري) for His saying, "and recite the Quran in a measured tone." Accordingly, what is meant by "celebrating the Name of the Lord" is the verbal remembrance (الذكر اللفظي) with the correspondence of the heart, and likewise, what is meant by "devotion" (التبتل) is devotion accompanied by the word.

It has been said that the verse is a generalization after specification, and what is meant by "remembrance" (الذكر) is the permanence of His remembrance, the Exalted, night and day, in whatever form it may be—of glorification (تسبيح), praise (تحميد), prayer, recitation of the Quran, and so forth. Remembrance was interpreted as "permanence" because he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not forget Him, the Exalted, such that he would be commanded to remember Him. What is meant is conventional permanence (الدوام العرفي) rather than actual permanence, due to its impossibility. [End of quote].

In this view, if he meant by remembrance the verbal remembrance, then his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) not forgetting his Lord, the Exalted, does not contradict his being commanded with verbal remembrance. If he meant what encompasses the remembrance of the heart (الذكر القلبي), then this is rejected. Even if it were granted, then firstly: his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) not forgetting his Lord up to the time of the address does not contradict his being commanded to remember Him after it. Secondly: his considering actual permanence impossible and interpreting permanence as conventional is a delusion arising from not grasping the meaning as it truly is. For Allah—majestic is His mention—is remembered by man and is not absent from him for even a moment, whether man is mindful of it or heedless of it. It is possible for Allah to make Himself known to him such that he is not heedless of Him even in any state. The Exalted said: فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُمْ لا يَسْأَمُونَ — ...those who are near your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire. (41:38), and He said: يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لا يَفْتُرُونَ — They glorify [Him] night and day, and they do not flag. (21:20). It has already been mentioned in the commentary on these two verses and the end of Surah al-A'raf that this is not exclusive to the angels.

In short, His saying, "And celebrate the Name of your Lord," is a command to remember one of His names or the name Allah (لفظ الجلالة) specifically. It has also been said that what is meant by it is the Basmalah (البسملة).

In His saying, "your Lord" (رَبِّكَ), there is a transition (التفات) from speaking in the first person plural in His saying, "Soon We shall cast" (إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي), to the third person. Perhaps the reason for this is to awaken the humility of servitude (ذلة العبودية), which is the bond between the servant and his Lord, by mentioning the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية).

His saying, وَتَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلاً — and devote yourself to Him with exclusive devotion (73:8). al-Tabattul (التبتل) has been explained as "detachment" (الانقطاع), meaning: "and detach yourself unto Allah." It is narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that al-tabattul is the raising of the hand to Allah and supplication (التضرع) to Him. This meaning is more suitable based on interpreting "remembrance" as verbal remembrance, as previously mentioned.

The word tabtila (تَبْتِيلاً) is apparently a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق). The apparent expectation was to say tabattula (تبتلا); the deviation to tabtila has been said to be because tabattala (تبتل) implies the meaning of battala (بتل). The meaning would then be: "and sever yourself from other than Him unto Him completely," or "compel yourself to raise the hand to Him and supplicate with compulsion." It has also been said it was for the sake of maintaining the verse endings (الفواصل).

His saying, the Exalted: رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَاتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلاً — The Lord of the east and the west; there is no god except Him; so take Him for your trustee. (73:9). This is a description detached from its descriptive function (وصف مقطوع عن الوصفية), the estimation being: "He is the Lord of the east and the west." The "Lord of the east and the west" is in the sense of the Lord of the entire world, for the east and the west are two relative directions that encompass all directions of the witnessed world. They were specified for mention due to the suitability of the preceding mention of night and day, which are linked to the rising and setting.

The reference to His Lordship, the Exalted, was not limited to the previous saying, "your Lord," in order to signal that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is commanded to take Him as a Lord because He is his Lord and the Lord of the entire world, not because He is his Lord alone—as a man among the idolaters might take an idol for himself alone, other than the idols taken by others. If his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) taking Him, the Exalted, as a Lord were of this sort, or if that were suspected, his call to monotheism (التوحيد) would not be valid.

Furthermore, it is so that His saying, "your Lord, the Lord of the east and the west"—which is in the sense of the Lord of the entire world—serves as a preamble and preparation for His subsequent saying, "there is no god except Him," by which He rationalizes the oneness of divinity (الألوهية). For divinity—which is the state of being worshipped—is among the branches of Lordship (الربوبية), which is ownership and governance, as has been mentioned repeatedly. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the only God, there is no god except Him, because He is the only Lord, there is no lord except Him.

His saying, فَاتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلاً — so take Him for your trustee (73:9), meaning in all your affairs. Appointing a trustee (توكيل الوكيل) is for a person to place another in his own stead, such that the other's will stands in place of his will and the other's action in place of his action. Thus, taking Him, the Exalted, as a trustee means that a person sees the entire matter as belonging to Him and returning to Him, the Exalted. As for external matters and cosmic events (الحوادث الكونية), it is that he does not see for himself, nor for any of the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية), any independence in influence (التأثير); for there is no influencer in existence in the true sense of influence except Allah. Thus, he does not attach himself to the influence of any cause with pleasure, anger, joy, regret, or otherwise. Rather, he seeks his goals and needs through the causes that Allah has made known, without relying on their independence in influence, and he refers the attainment of the objective to Allah so that He may choose for him what He is pleased with.

As for matters related to action, such as acts of worship (العبادات) and transactions (المعاملات), it is that he makes his will follow the legislative will (الإرادة التشريعية) of his Lord; thus, he acts according to what Allah, the Exalted, wants from him in what He has legislated of the Law (الشريعة).

From here, it becomes clear that His saying, "so take Him for your trustee," is connected to His saying, "And celebrate the Name of your Lord," etc., and the legislative commands preceding it, just as it is connected to what follows it of His saying, "And be patient," His saying, "forsake them," and His saying, "And leave Me."

His saying, the Exalted: وَاصْبِرْ عَلى ما يَقُولُونَ وَاهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْراً جَمِيلاً — And be patient with what they say, and forsake them with a graceful parting. (73:10). This and what follows it are coordinated with the object of the fa (الفاء) in His saying, "so take Him for your trustee." The meaning is: Take Him as a trustee, and the necessity of taking Him as a trustee is that you be patient with what they say of that which contains harm to you, mockery of you, and accusing you of what is not in you—such as their saying: "He has fabricated against Allah," "a soothsayer," "a poet," "a madman," "fables of the ancients," and other things that the Quran recounts.

And that you forsake them with a "graceful parting" (هجرا جميلا). What is meant by the graceful parting, according to what the context provides, is to treat them with good character and to call to the truth with sincere advice, and not to meet their speech with what is in one's power of retaliation in kind. The verse does not contradict the verse of fighting (آية القتال); thus, there is no basis for the saying of those who claim it was abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of fighting.

His saying, the Exalted: وَذَرْنِي وَالْمُكَذِّبِينَ أُولِي النَّعْمَةِ وَمَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلاً — And leave Me to the impugners, the possessors of ease and plenty, and give them a little respite. (73:11). This is a threat to the disbelievers. It is said: "Leave me and so-and-so" (دعني وفلانا) and "Leave Me and so-and-so" (ذرني وفلانا), meaning: Do not come between Me and him until I take vengeance upon him.

What is meant by "the impugners, the possessors of ease and plenty" (المكذبين أولي النعمة) are the disbelievers mentioned in the previous verse, or their followed leaders. The combination of describing them as "impugners" and describing them as "possessors of ease and plenty" is to point to the cause of the punishment He threatens them with; for their impugning the divine call while they are enjoying the bounty of their Lord is ingratitude (كفران) on their part for the bounty, and the recompense for ingratitude is the removal of the bounty and its replacement with vengeance (النقمة).

What is meant by the "little" (قَلِيلاً) for which they are given respite is the short time they remain on earth until they return to their Lord, whereupon He will call them to account and recompense them. The Exalted said: إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُ بَعِيداً وَنَراهُ قَرِيباً — Indeed they see it to be far off, and We see it to be near. (70:6-7), and He said: مَتاعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمِهادُ — A brief enjoyment; then their abode will be hell, and it is an evil resting place. (3:197).

The verse in its apparent meaning is general. It has been said it is a threat to them with the Battle of Badr, but this is not apparent. In the verse, there is a transition (التفات) from the third person in "your Lord" to the first person singular in "leave Me" (ذَرْنِي). Perhaps the reason for this is to intensify the threat by attributing the matter to Him, the Glorified, Himself. Then He transitioned in His saying, "Indeed, with Us" (إِنَّ لَدَيْنا), to the first person plural to indicate grandeur (العظمة).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ لَدَيْنا أَنْكالاً وَجَحِيماً — Indeed with Us are heavy fetters and a fierce fire (73:12). This is the rationale for His saying, "And leave Me," etc. al-Ankal (الأنكال) are fetters. al-Raghib said: It is said "he recoiled (نكل) from the thing" when he became weak and incapable; "I fettered him" (نكلته) means I bound him. al-Nikl (النكل)—with a kasrah then a sukun—is the animal's tether and the iron of the bit because they are both preventers; the plural is al-ankal. [End of quote]. He also said: al-Jahmah (الجحمة) is the intensity of the fire's blazing, and from it comes al-jahim (الجحيم). [End of quote].

His saying, the Exalted: وَطَعاماً ذا غُصَّةٍ وَعَذاباً أَلِيماً — and a food that chokes, and a painful punishment (73:13). It is said in al-Majma': al-Ghussah (الغصة) is the hesitation of the morsel in the throat such that the eater cannot swallow it. It is said: "He choked (غص) on his saliva," and "In his heart is a ghussah from such-and-such," which is like a sting with which food and drink cannot be swallowed. [End of quote].

The two verses mention the vengeances of the Hereafter which have replaced the bounties of the world as a recompense for their ingratitude for the bounties of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الْأَرْضُ وَالْجِبالُ وَكانَتِ الْجِبالُ كَثِيباً مَهِيلاً — the day the earth and the mountains shall quake, and the mountains shall be like dunes of shifting sand. (73:14). This is the time (ظرف) for the punishment promised in the two preceding verses. al-Raghib said: al-Rajf (الرجف) is intense agitation; it is said "the earth and the sea quaked." [End of quote]. In al-Majma': al-Kathib (الكثيب) is abundant, gathered sand; "I poured it" (هلت أهيله هيلا) so it is "poured" (مهيل) when its bottom is moved and its top flows down. [End of quote]. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ أَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ رَسُولاً شاهِداً عَلَيْكُمْ كَما أَرْسَلْنا إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ رَسُولاً — Indeed We have sent to you an apostle to be a witness to you, just as We sent an apostle to Pharaoh. (73:15). This is a warning to the impugners, the possessors of ease and plenty from his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) people, after He had threatened the absolute impugners, the possessors of ease and plenty, with what He has prepared for them of punishment on the Day of Resurrection—by comparing their state to the state of Pharaoh, who was arrogant toward Allah and His Messenger and who sought to humiliate the Messenger of Allah and those who believed with him from his people. Then He struck their ears with what the matter of Pharaoh ended in—of Allah's seizing him with a "terrible seizing" (أخذا وبيلا)—so let them take heed and take their precaution.

In the verse, there is a transition (التفات) from the third person to the second person address. It is as if the Speaker, when He threatened them with punishment in the third person, was moved by concern for those impugners because of how they cast themselves with their own hands into eternal destruction due to the foolishness of their opinion; so He addressed them directly with the warning so that any doubt or hesitation might be removed from their souls, the proof (الحجة) might be completed against them, and perhaps they might be God-conscious (يتقون). For this reason, He followed the comparison of them to Pharaoh, the comparison of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Moses (عليه‌السلام), and the reference to the punishment of Pharaoh's matter with His saying: "So how will you guard yourselves, if you disbelieve, against a day..." etc.

So His saying, "Indeed We have sent to you an apostle to be a witness to you," is a reference to the confirmation of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) messengership from Him, the Exalted, and his witnessing over their deeds by bearing them in the world and delivering them on the Day of Resurrection. The discussion regarding the meaning of the "witnessing of deeds" (شهادة الأعمال) has already been presented repeatedly in the verses containing it. In the reference to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) witnessing, there is a type of deterrent for them against disobeying him, opposing him, and impugning him.

His saying, "just as We sent an apostle to Pharaoh," refers to Moses son of Imran (عليه‌السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: فَعَصى فِرْعَوْنُ الرَّسُولَ فَأَخَذْناهُ أَخْذاً وَبِيلاً — But Pharaoh disobeyed the apostle, so We seized him with a terrible seizing. (73:16), meaning intense and heavy.

This is a reference to the end of Pharaoh's matter in his disobedience to Moses (عليه‌السلام). In expressing Moses as "the apostle" (الرسول), there is a reference to the fact that the cause necessitating the seizing of Pharaoh was his opposition to the matter of his messengership, not Moses himself as Moses. If the cause is the opposition to the messengership, then let them beware of opposing the messengership of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

Likewise, placing the explicit noun in place of the pronoun in His saying, "But Pharaoh disobeyed," is to intimate that what he possessed of might, loftiness in the earth, and boasting of the abundance of equipment, the vastness of the kingdom, and the influence of will, did not avail him anything and did not ward off the punishment of Allah from him—so what is the thought regarding these impugners? They are, as Allah said: جُنْدٌ ما هُنالِكَ مَهْزُومٌ مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ — [They are] a defeated host of the factions [assembled] there. (38:11).

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَيْفَ تَتَّقُونَ إِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ يَوْماً يَجْعَلُ الْوِلْدانَ شِيباً — So how will you guard yourselves, if you disbelieve, against a day which will make the children hoary-headed? (73:17). The attribution of "guarding" (الاتقاء) to the "day" is a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي), and what is meant is guarding against the punishment promised therein. Accordingly, "a day" (يوما) is the direct object of "you guard yourselves" (تتقون).

It has been said: the object of "you guard yourselves" is omitted and "a day" is an adverb of time for it, the estimation being: "So how will you guard yourselves against the punishment existing in a day." It has also been said: the object is omitted and "a day" is an adverb of time for the guarding. Other things have also been said.

His saying, يَجْعَلُ الْوِلْدانَ شِيباً — which will make the children hoary-headed (73:17). al-Shib (الشيب) is the plural of ashyab (أشيب), the opposite of "youth" (الشاب). Making the children hoary-headed is a metaphor for the intensity of the day, not for its length.

His saying, the Exalted: السَّماءُ مُنْفَطِرٌ بِهِ كانَ وَعْدُهُ مَفْعُولاً — whereat the sky will be rent asunder? His promise is bound to be fulfilled. (73:18). This is a reference after a reference to the intensity of the day. al-Infitar (الانفطار) is splitting. The masculinity of the attribute is because "the sky" (السماء) is permissible in both genders—it is treated as masculine and feminine. The pronoun in "whereat" (بِهِ) refers to the day, and the ba (الباء) is in the sense of "in" or for causality (السببية). The meaning is: The sky is split in that day or because of that day—meaning because of its intensity.

His saying, كانَ وَعْدُهُ مَفْعُولاً — His promise is bound to be fulfilled (73:18), is a resumption (استئناف) to record what has preceded of the threat and that it is a decreed certainty. The attribution of the promise to His pronoun, the Exalted, is perhaps to signal that none is fit for this promise except Allah, the Exalted; thus, the pronoun suffices without the need to mention Him by His name.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً — This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way to his Lord. (73:19). The reference with "this" (هذِهِ) is to the preceding verses with what they contain of striking warnings and deterrents. The "reminder" (التذكرة) is the exhortation (الموعظة) by which one is reminded of what one should act upon.

His saying, "So let anyone who wishes" (فَمَنْ شاءَ): the object of "wishes" is omitted. It is well-known in such a case that the object is estimated from the genus of the response, and the context suits it. The estimation is: "So let anyone who wishes to take a way to his Lord, take [it]," etc. It has been said: the estimated object is "taking heed" (الاتعاظ). What is meant by "taking the way to Him" is taking the way to proximity to Him; the way is faith (الإيمان) and obedience (الطاعة). This is what the commentators have mentioned.

It is possible that "this" is a reference to what preceded in the beginning of the Surah of the verses inviting to standing the night and keeping vigil therein. The verse is aimed at expanding the address and generalizing it to other than the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from the believers, after the address at the beginning of the Surah was specific to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The evidence for this generalization is His saying, "So let anyone who wishes," etc.

What we have mentioned is supported by the occurrence of this very verse, "This is indeed a reminder," etc., in Surah al-Dahr after the night prayer was referred to by His saying, the Exalted: وَسَبِّحْهُ لَيْلاً طَوِيلاً — ...and glorify Him for a long [part] of the night. (76:26). It is concluded from this that the night prayer is a specific path (سبيل خاصة) that guides the servant to his Lord.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Bazzar, al-Tabarani in al-Awsat, and Abu Nu'aym in al-Dala'il narrated from Jabir who said: Quraysh gathered in the Assembly House (دار الندوة) and said: "Give this man a name by which people will turn away from him." They said: "A soothsayer (كاهن)." They said: "He is not a soothsayer." They said: "A madman (مجنون)." They said: "He is not a madman." They said: "A sorcerer (ساحر)." They said: "He is not a sorcerer." They said: "He separates between a lover and his beloved." So the polytheists dispersed upon that.

This reached the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so he wrapped himself in his garments (تزمل في ثيابه) and covered himself in them (تدثر فيها). Then Gabriel came to him and said: "O you wrapped up! (يا أيها المزمل) O you covered up! (يا أيها المدثر)."

I say: The end of the narration is not free from issues, as its apparent meaning is the simultaneous revelation of the two Surahs. Furthermore, the Quran, even in Surah al-Muddaththir, recounts their naming him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with evil titles such as soothsayer, sorcerer, madman, and poet, but it does not mention therein their saying: "He separates between a lover and his beloved."

In the same source: Abdullah ibn Ahmad in Kitab al-Zuhd and Muhammad ibn Nasr in Kitab al-Salah narrated from Aisha who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would seldom sleep during the night when Allah said to him: 'Stand vigil through the night, except a little.'"

In al-Kashshaf, from Aisha: she was asked: "What was his wrapping (تزميله)?" She said: "It was a mantle (مرط) whose length was fourteen cubits—half of it was over me while I was sleeping, and half of it was over him while he was praying." She was asked: "What was it [made of]?" She said: "By Allah, it was neither raw silk (خزا) nor fine silk (قزا), nor fine wool (مرعزيا), nor silk (إبريسما), nor wool (صوفا). Its warp (سداه) was hair and its weft (لحمته) was fur."

I say: The narration is suspected of being fabricated (بالوضع), for the Surah is among the ancient ones revealed in Mecca, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only consummated his marriage with Aisha in Medina after the Migration (الهجرة).

From Jawami' al-Jami': It is narrated that he entered upon Khadija having been overcome by trembling (جئث فرقا) and said: "Wrap me up (زملوني)." While he was in that state, Gabriel called him: "O you wrapped up! (يا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abd ibn Humayd, ibn Jarir, and ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Sa'id ibn Jubayr who said: "When 'O you wrapped up! Stand vigil through the night, except a little' was revealed, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained in this state for ten years—standing the night as Allah commanded him—and a group of his companions would stand with him. Then Allah revealed after ten years: 'Indeed your Lord knows that you stand...'—to His saying—'and maintain the prayer.' So Allah mitigated for them after ten years."

I say: The revelation of the verse of mitigation (آية التخفيف) has also been narrated as occurring after one year, and it has also been narrated as occurring after eight months. The night prayer was not obligatory upon other than the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "This is indeed a reminder," as previously mentioned. This is supported by what is in the narration: "and a group of his companions."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام), he said: I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: "Stand vigil through the night, except a little." He said: "Allah commanded him to pray every night—unless a night among the nights comes upon him in which he does not pray anything."

I say: The narration points to one of the views regarding the verse.

In al-Majma': It has been said that "its half" (نصفه) is an appositive for "the little" (القليل), so it would be an exposition of the excepted part. This view is supported by what is narrated from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) who said: "The little is the half, or decrease from the little a little—or add to the little a little."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Askari in al-Mawa'iz narrated from Ali (عليه‌السلام) that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was asked about the saying of Allah: "and recite the Quran in a measured tone." He said: "Articulate it clearly (بينه تبيينا); do not scatter it like the scattering of dry dates (الدقل), and do not recite it rapidly like the rapid recitation of poetry. Stop at its wonders, move the hearts with it, and let not the concern of any of you be the end of the Surah."

I say: This meaning is also narrated in Usul al-Kafi, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sulayman from al-Sadiq from Ali (عليه‌السلام), and the wording is: "Articulate it clearly; do not recite it rapidly like the rapid recitation of poetry, and do not scatter it like the scattering of sand. But empty your hardened hearts—and let not the concern of any of you be the end of the Surah."

In the same source: ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Tawus who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was asked: "Which of the people has the best recitation?" He said: "The one who, when you hear him reciting, you see that he fears Allah."

In Usul al-Kafi, with its chain to Ali ibn Abi Hamza, he said: Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) said: "Indeed, the Quran is not to be read with babbling (هذرمة), but it is to be recited with measured recitation (ترتيلا). So when you pass by a verse in which there is mention of Paradise, stop at it—and ask Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, for Paradise. And when you pass by a verse in which there is mention of the Fire, stop at it—and seek refuge with Allah from the Fire."

In al-Majma', regarding the meaning of measured recitation (الترتيل), from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), he said: "It is that you linger over it and beautify your voice with it."

In the same source: It is narrated from Umm Salama that she said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to break his recitation verse by verse."

In the same source: From Anas who said: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to prolong his voice with a drawl (مدا)."

In the same source: al-Harith ibn Hisham asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), saying: "O Messenger of Allah, how does the revelation come to you?" The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Sometimes it comes to me like the ringing of a bell—and that is the hardest upon me—then it departs (يفصم) from me while I have grasped what he said. And sometimes the angel takes the form of a man, and I grasp what he says."

Aisha said: "Revelation would be sent down to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he was on his mount, and it would strike its chest (بجرانها) [against the ground]."

She said: "And I have seen it descending upon him on an intensely cold day—then it would depart from him while his forehead would surely stream with sweat."

From Tafsir al-Ayyashi, with its chain to Isa ibn Ubayd from his father from his grandfather from Ali (عليه‌السلام), he said: "The Quran used to abrogate (ينسخ) some parts of it with others, and what is taken from the matter of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the latter part."

"And among the last of what was revealed to him was Surah al-Ma'idah—it abrogated what was before it and nothing abrogated it. It was revealed to him while he was on a grey mule—and the revelation weighed heavily upon it until it stopped—and its belly hung down until I saw its navel almost touching the ground."

I say: If the narration is authentic, the appearance of the effect of the weight of revelation upon the camel or the mule is of the nature of the embodiment of meanings (تجسم المعاني), and much like it is found in what is transmitted of miracles (المعجزات) and the wonders of the saints (كرامات الأولياء). As for the attribution of material weight to revelation—which is speech—it is inconceivable.

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Hisham ibn Salim from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "Indeed the watch of the night is more effective and more conducive to right speech." He said: "He means by His saying: 'and more conducive to right speech' (وَأَقْوَمُ قِيلاً) the standing of a man from his bed desiring thereby Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and desiring none other than Him."

I say: It was also narrated with two other chains in al-Tahdhib and al-Ilal from Hisham from him (عليه‌السلام).

In al-Majma' regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed the watch of the night..." the verse: It is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) that they both said: "It is the standing in the last part of the night."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Husayn ibn Ali that he was seen praying between Maghrib and Isha—so he was asked about that? He said: "Indeed, they are both from the watch (الناشئة)."

In al-Majma' regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and devote yourself to Him with exclusive devotion" (وَتَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلاً): Muhammad ibn Muslim, Zurarah, and Humran narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) that "this devotion (التبتل) is the raising of the two hands in the prayer." In the narration of Abu Basir, he said: "It is the raising of your hand to Allah and your supplication (تضرعك)."

I say: This applies to the qunut (قنوت) of the prayer. In a narration: "It is the raising of the two hands and the moving of the two index fingers." In a narration: "The gesturing with the finger." In a narration: "The supplication with one finger with which he points."

In the same source regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and a food that chokes" (وَطَعاماً ذا غُصَّةٍ), the verse: From Abdullah ibn Umar: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) heard a reciter reciting this, and he fainted (صعق)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi regarding His saying: "and the mountains shall be like dunes of shifting sand" (وَكانَتِ الْجِبالُ كَثِيباً مَهِيلاً), he said: "Like sand that slides down."

Ayah

۞ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ أَدْنَىٰ مِن ثُلُثَىِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَنِصْفَهُۥ وَثُلُثَهُۥ وَطَآئِفَةٌ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مَعَكَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُقَدِّرُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ عَلِمَ أَن لَّن تُحْصُوهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَٱقْرَءُوا۟ مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ عَلِمَ أَن سَيَكُونُ مِنكُم مَّرْضَىٰ ۙ وَءَاخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَبْتَغُونَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ ۙ وَءَاخَرُونَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَٱقْرَءُوا۟ مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ ۚ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَقْرِضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا ۚ وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرًا وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْرًا ۚ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌۢ (٢٠)
Indeed your Lord knows that you stand vigil for nearly two thirds of the night—or [at times] a half or a third of it—along with a group of those who are with you. Allah measures the night and the day. He knows that you cannot calculate it [exactly], and so He was lenient toward you. So recite as much of the Quran as is feasible. He knows that some of you will be sick, while others will travel in the land seeking Allah’s bounty, and yet others will fight in the way of Allah. So recite as much of it as is feasible, and maintain the prayer and pay the zakat and lend Allah a good loan. Whatever good you send ahead for your souls you will find it with Allah [in a form] that is better and greater with respect to reward. And plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (20)

(Commentary)

This verse is based on granting a concession (التخفيف) regarding what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded at the beginning of the Surah concerning the night vigil (قيام الليل) and praying therein. The ruling was then generalized to the rest of the believers by His saying: إِنَّ هَذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ "Indeed this is a reminder" (73:19).

The tone of the verse is one of concession (التخفيف) by allowing whatever is easy of the Quran, without abrogating (نسخ) the essence of the previous ruling that prohibited abandoning the night vigil for two-thirds of the night, or half of it, or a third of it.

It has been mentioned in more than one report that the verse is Meccan (مكية), having been revealed eight months, a year, or ten years after the revelation of the verses at the beginning of the Surah. However, this view is weakened by the fact that the verse includes His saying, the Exalted: وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكاةَ وَأَقْرِضُوا اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً "and maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and lend Allah a good loan" (73:20). The apparent meaning of "zakat" (الزكاة) — mentioned as it is after prayer and before recommended spending — is the obligatory zakat (الزكاة المفروضة), and the zakat was only made obligatory in Medina after the Migration (الهجرة).

The claim of some that zakat was made obligatory in Mecca without the determination of the taxable limits (الأنصباء), and that what was ordained in Medina was merely the determination of those limits, is an arbitrary assertion (تحكم) without evidence. Similarly, the claim of others that it is possible the verse is among those whose ruling was delayed after its revelation is also unsubstantiated.

Furthermore, the verse mentions fighting (القتال), as He says: وَآخَرُونَ يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ "and others who fight in the way of Allah" (73:20). It was not in the interest of the true mission at that time and in those circumstances for any mention of fighting to occur within its text in any way whatsoever. Thus, it is evident that the verse is Medinan (مدنية) and not Meccan, a view toward which some scholars have inclined.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ أَدْنى مِنْ ثُلُثَيِ اللَّيْلِ وَنِصْفَهُ وَثُلُثَهُ Indeed your Lord knows that you stand [in prayer] nearly two-thirds of the night, and [sometimes] half of it, and [sometimes] a third of it (73:20) until the end of the verse. The address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The use of the expression "your Lord" (ربك) alludes to the encompassment of divine mercy and care. Likewise, His saying: "He knows that you stand..." (يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ) carries an air of appreciation (الشكر), as the Exalted says: وَكانَ سَعْيُكُمْ مَشْكُوراً "and your endeavor has been appreciated" (76:22).

His saying: "nearly two-thirds of the night, and half of it, and a third of it" (تَقُومُ أَدْنى مِنْ ثُلُثَيِ اللَّيْلِ وَنِصْفَهُ وَثُلُثَهُ). The word adna (أدنى) is a superlative (اسم تفضيل) derived from dunuww (الدنو), meaning closeness. Customary usage (العرف) applies adna to that which is close to something but slightly less than it. For example, it is said their number is adna than ten if they are nine, but not if they are eleven. Thus, the meaning of "nearly two-thirds of the night" (أَدْنى مِنْ ثُلُثَيِ اللَّيْلِ) is closer to two-thirds and slightly less.

The coordinating conjunction "and" (الواو) in His saying: "and half of it, and a third of it" (وَنِصْفَهُ وَثُلُثَهُ) is for absolute conjunction (مطلق الجمع). The meaning is that He knows you stand in some nights nearly two-thirds of the night, in some half of it, and in some a third of it.

His saying: وَطائِفَةٌ مِنَ الَّذِينَ مَعَكَ "and a group of those who are with you" (73:20). The intended "with-ness" (المعية) is in faith (الإيمان), and the word "of" (من) denotes partitivity (التبعيض). Thus, the verse indicates that some of them used to perform the night vigil just as the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did. It has been said that "of" (من) is explicative (بيانية), but this is as you see [unlikely].

His saying: وَاللهُ يُقَدِّرُ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ "and Allah appoints the measure of the night and the day" (73:20) serves as a justification (التعليل) for His saying: "Indeed your Lord knows" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ). The meaning is: how could He not know when He is Allah, to Whom belong creation and measuring (التقدير)? For in determining the measure of the night and the day lies the determination of their third, half, and two-thirds. The attribution of measuring the night and day to the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة) rather than the name "the Lord" or others is because measuring is among the affairs of creation (الخلق), and creation belongs to Allah, to Whom everything ultimately returns.

His saying: عَلِمَ أَنْ لَنْ تُحْصُوهُ فَتابَ عَلَيْكُمْ فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ He knows that you cannot keep count of it, so He has turned to you [clemently]. So recite as much of the Quran as is feasible (73:20). Keeping count (الإحصاء) means obtaining the measure of a thing and its number, and encompassing it. The pronoun in "you cannot keep count of it" (لَنْ تُحْصُوهُ) refers to the measure (التقدير) or to standing for the measure of a third, half, or nearly two-thirds. Keeping count of that, given the variation of nights in length and shortness throughout the days of the year, is something not feasible (لا يتيسر) for the generality of those charged with religious duties (المكلفين). It becomes even more difficult for one who sleeps at the beginning of the night and intends to stand for one of the three measures without taking the precaution of standing the entire night or what is equivalent to it.

Thus, the meaning of His saying: "He knows that you cannot keep count of it" (عَلِمَ أَنْ لَنْ تُحْصُوهُ) is His knowledge, the Exalted, that keeping count of the measure they were commanded to stand of the night is not feasible for the generality of the subjects.

The meaning of His saying: "so He has turned to you [clemently]" (فَتابَ عَلَيْكُمْ) is His turning (توبته) and returning to them in the sense of the inclination of divine mercy (انعطاف الرحمة الإلهية) toward them with concession (التخفيف). For Allah, the Glorified, has a "turning" toward His servants by extending His mercy over them, the effect of which is granting them success in repentance (التوبة), or in absolute obedience (الطاعة), or the lifting of some obligations, or granting concession. The Exalted says: ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا "then He turned to them [clemently] so that they might repent" (9:118).

Just as He has a "turning" toward them in the sense of returning to them after their repentance, the effect of which is the forgiveness of their sins, as has been previously indicated.

The meaning of His saying: "So recite as much of the Quran as is feasible" (فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ) is the concession (التخفيف) in the night vigil regarding the duration for the generality of the subjects, as a consequence of His knowledge that they would not be able to keep count of it.

The necessary implication of this is an expansion (التوسعة) in the obligation of the night vigil regarding the duration, so that it accommodates the generality of the subjects for whom keeping count is difficult. This is not abrogation (النسخ) in the sense that standing for a third, half, or nearly two-thirds for one who is able to do so becomes a forbidden innovation (بدعة). This is because the aforementioned keeping count is only not feasible for the subjects as a whole (مجموع المكلفين), not for every single one of them. If it were impossible for all of them and not feasible for any one of them, it would not have been legislated from the beginning, for Allah does not charge any soul except within its capacity.

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, affirms for His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a group of those with him the standing of a third, half, and nearly two-thirds, while attributing the inability to keep count to the whole group—who are inevitably the ones standing and others. Thus, the ruling was only difficult for the group as a collective (المجموع من حيث المجموع), not for every individual. So He expanded the obligation by His saying: "So recite as much of the Quran as is feasible" (فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ) and eased the matter with concession so that the generality of the subjects might have a share in it, while the original ruling contained in the beginning of the Surah remains as it is for whoever is able to keep count and desires to do so. The ruling is recommended (استحبابي) for the rest of the believers, even if the apparent address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) implies obligation (الوجوب), as previously indicated.

The commentators have various opinions regarding whether the intended "standing of the night" (قيام الليل) is prayer therein or reciting the Quran outside of prayer; and on the first view, whether it was obligatory (واجب) upon the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers, or recommended (مستحب) for all, or obligatory for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and recommended for others; and then regarding the abrogation of the ruling by the concession of "what is feasible" in this verse, or the replacement of prayer with the recitation of what is feasible of the Quran. These are opinions in which there is not much benefit in addressing or investigating.

His saying: عَلِمَ أَنْ سَيَكُونُ مِنْكُمْ مَرْضى وَآخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَبْتَغُونَ مِنْ فَضْلِ اللهِ وَآخَرُونَ يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ He knows that there will be some among you who are sick, and others who travel in the land seeking Allah’s grace, and others who fight in the way of Allah (73:20). This is an indication of another wisdom (مصلحة) necessitating concession in the matter of standing for a third, half, or nearly two-thirds of the night, beyond it being difficult for the generality of the subjects in the manner first described. For the aforementioned keeping count for the sick, the traveler, and the fighter—given their respective conditions—is extremely difficult and arduous.

The meaning of "traveling in the land" (الضرب في الأرض) to seek Allah's grace is seeking sustenance (الرزق) by traveling from land to land for trade.

His saying: فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكاةَ وَأَقْرِضُوا اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً So recite as much of it as is feasible, and maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and lend Allah a good loan (73:20). This is a repetition of the concession for emphasis (تأكيد). The pronoun in "of it" (منه) refers to the Quran. The meaning is to perform the prayer according to the capacity of the time in which they stand.

The "prayer" (الصلاة) commanded to be maintained is the obligatory prayer (الفريضة). If the verse is Medinan, it refers to the five daily prayers; if it is Meccan, it is according to whatever prayer was then obligatory. "Zakat" (الزكاة) refers to the obligatory zakat (الزكاة المفروضة). "Lending to Allah" (إقراضه تعالى) refers to financial expenditures in the way of Allah other than zakat.

The conjunction of the command to maintain prayer, pay zakat, and lend is to allude to the fact that religious obligations (التكاليف الدينية) remain as they are in the necessity of being concerned with them and attending to their affair. Let no one imagine that concession and leniency (المسامحة) spread to all obligations. Thus, the verse is similar to His saying in the verse of secret conversation (آية النجوى): فَإِذْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا وَتابَ اللهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكاةَ وَأَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ "Since you did not do it and Allah has turned to you [clemently], maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and obey Allah and His Apostle" (58:13).

His saying: وَما تُقَدِّمُوا لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِنْدَ اللهِ هُوَ خَيْراً وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْراً Whatever good you send ahead for your souls you will find it with Allah [in a form] that is better and greater in reward (73:20). The phrase "of good" (مِنْ خَيْرٍ) is an explanation (بيان) for the relative pronoun. "Good" (الخير) refers to absolute obedience (الطاعة), encompassing both the obligatory and the recommended. The word "it" (هُوَ) is a separating pronoun (ضمير فصل) or an emphasis for the pronoun in "you will find it" (تجدوه).

The meaning is: the obedience that you send ahead for your souls—meaning to live by it in the Hereafter—you will find it with Allah—meaning on the Day of Meeting—better than everything you do or leave behind, and greater in reward.

His saying: وَاسْتَغْفِرُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ And plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful (73:20). He concluded the speech with the command to seek forgiveness (الاستغفار). In His saying: "Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ), there is an intimation of a promise of forgiveness and mercy. It is not unlikely that the intended meaning of seeking forgiveness is the performance of absolute acts of obedience, because they are means (وسائل) by which one seeks Allah's forgiveness; thus, performing them is a form of seeking forgiveness.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the report of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ أَدْنى مِنْ ثُلُثَيِ اللَّيْلِ وَنِصْفَهُ وَثُلُثَهُ — "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did that and gave the people glad tidings of it, but it became difficult for them. And عَلِمَ أَنْ لَنْ تُحْصُوهُ (He knows that you cannot keep count of it) — a man would stand and not know when the half of the night occurred, nor when the two-thirds occurred. A man would stand until morning out of fear that he might not have preserved the [required] measure."

"So Allah revealed: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ until His saying عَلِمَ أَنْ لَنْ تُحْصُوهُ — He says: [regarding] when the half and the third occur, this verse abrogated [the previous one with] فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ (So recite as much of the Quran as is feasible). And know that no prophet ever came except that he secluded himself with the night prayer, and no prophet ever came with the night prayer at the beginning of the night."

I say: The gist of the narration is that the beginning of the Surah makes the night prayer obligatory and its end abrogates it. Something similar has been narrated through Sunni channels from Ibn Abbas and others, and what pertains to this has been discussed in the preceding commentary.

In al-Majma', al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim Ibrahim al-Haskani narrated with his chain from al-Kalbi from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَطائِفَةٌ مِنَ الَّذِينَ مَعَكَ (and a group of those who are with you), he said: "Ali and Abu Dharr."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ (So recite as much of it as is feasible), it is narrated from Imam al-Rida from his father from his grandfather (peace be upon them) who said: "Whatever is feasible for you therein of humility of the heart (خشوع القلب) and purity of the inner secret (صفاء السر)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ, he said: "One hundred verses."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abdullah ibn Mas'ud who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'There is no importer who brings food to one of the lands of the Muslims and sells it at the price of the day, except that his station with Allah is that of a martyr.' Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: وَآخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَبْتَغُونَ مِنْ فَضْلِ اللهِ وَآخَرُونَ يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ (and others who travel in the land seeking Allah’s grace, and others who fight in the way of Allah)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with his chain from Zur'ah from Sama'ah, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: وَأَقْرِضُوا اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً (and lend Allah a good loan). He said: 'It is other than the zakat.'"

In al-Khصال, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in the "Hadith of the Four Hundred": "Seek forgiveness (الاستغفار) frequently to draw sustenance (الرزق), and send ahead whatever you can of righteous deeds (عمل الخير), for you shall find it tomorrow."

I say: The latter part is taken from His saying, the Exalted: وَما تُقَدِّمُوا لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِنْدَ اللهِ هُوَ خَيْراً وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْراً (Whatever good you send ahead for your souls you will find it with Allah [in a form] that is better and greater in reward).
